coredata.dc:title: Deep eutectic solvent assisted synthesis of carbon dots using Sophora flavescens Aiton modified with polyethyleneimine: Application in myricetin sensing and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Min
Kang, Xun
Deng, Linlin
Wang, Min
Xia, Zhining
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: Here, an efficient method for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) using a deep eutectic solvent (DES) was developed. To investigate the influence of different DESs on the quantum yield of CDs, different hydrogen-bonding acceptors (HBAs) and hydrogen-bonding donors (HBDs) were used to synthesize the DES and prepare CDs. Using Sophora flavescens Aiton as precursor, CDs were prepared using choline chloride (ChCl)/urea based DES as reaction media and doping agent in the presence of water. The CDs showed strong blue fluorescence and were further modified with polyethyleneimine (CDs@PEI). The fluorescence intensity of CDs@PEI was selectively quenched by myricetin with a limit of detection (LOD) of 10 nM. Furthermore, CDs@PEI was used to analyze myricetin in the extracts that were fluorescent by DES with satisfactory performance of Abelmoschus manihot (Linn.) Medicus flowers, vine teas and blueberries. Finally, the bio-imaging application of CDs@PEI was tested and the results confirmed its potential application in bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Deep eutectic solvents
Sophora flavescens Aiton
Polyethyleneimine
Myricetin
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of CDs and CDs@PEI in living cells
Cell imaging of CDs and CDs@PEI
UV–vis absorption and fluorescence properties of CDs and CDs@PEI
Effect of myricetin on the fluorescence properties of CDs and CDs@PEI
Possible quenching mechanism of the CDs@PEI - myricetin system
Selectivity study of CDs and CDs@PEI
Determination of myricetin in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21904109 and No. 21974015 ) and the Luzhou Science and Technology Bureau Innovation Miao sub-project (NO. 2019-RCM-92).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of CDs (A) and CDs@PEI (B). C. Particle size distribution of CDs@PEI. D.EDS spectra of CDs@PEI.

A. UV–vis absorption of CDs and CDs@PEI. B. Fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of CDs@PEI. The insets show the images of CDs@PEI under daylight and under 365 nm UV light. C. Excitation dependent emission spectra of CDs@PEI. D. Normalized emission spectra of CDs@PEI with different excitation wavelength. E. Image of CIE coordinates showing the blue color of CDs@PEI.

A. Changes in fluorescence emission intensity of CDs@PEI in the presence of different concentrations of myricetin (0–100 μM).B. Linear correlation of F0/F versus myricetin concentrations for CDs and CDs@PEI.

A. Fluorescence lifetime of the CDs@PEI and CDs@PEI/myricetin. B. Stern-Volmer values at different temperatures. C.UV–vis absorption band of myricetin, the excitation band and emission band of the CDs@PEI.

A. Cytotoxicity determination of CDs and CDs@PEI in HepG2 cells at different concentration. B. Images of HepG2 cells in bright-field. C. Fluorescence image of cells after incubation with CDs for 4 h. D. Fluorescence image of cells after incubation with CDs@PEI for 4 h.
Schematic presentation of synthesis and application of CDs and CDs@PEI.
Recovery of myricetin in real samples.

row.entry: Initial myricetin amount determined by HPLC (μM)
Initial myricetin amount determined by CDs@PEG(μM)

head.ce:title: Deep eutectic solvent assisted synthesis of carbon dots using Sophora flavescens Aiton modified with polyethyleneimine: Application in myricetin sensing and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, an efficient method for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) using a deep eutectic solvent (DES) was developed. To investigate the influence of different DESs on the quantum yield of CDs, different hydrogen-bonding acceptors (HBAs) and hydrogen-bonding donors (HBDs) were used to synthesize the DES and prepare CDs. Using Sophora flavescens Aiton as precursor, CDs were prepared using choline chloride (ChCl)/urea based DES as reaction media and doping agent in the presence of water. The CDs showed strong blue fluorescence and were further modified with polyethyleneimine (CDs@PEI). The fluorescence intensity of CDs@PEI was selectively quenched by myricetin with a limit of detection (LOD) of 10 nM. Furthermore, CDs@PEI was used to analyze myricetin in the extracts that were fluorescent by DES with satisfactory performance of Abelmoschus manihot (Linn.) Medicus flowers, vine teas and blueberries. Finally, the bio-imaging application of CDs@PEI was tested and the results confirmed its potential application in bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are one kind of outstanding functional materials with excellent photoluminescence properties (Li et al., 2020). Most CDs possess desirable properties such as low cost, good water solubility, good biocompatibility, low bio-toxicity, and tunable surface chemistry (Li, Zhao, Li, & Gao, 2020). Thus, they have been widely applied in the fields of sensing (Lv, Man, Dong, Huang, & Wang, 2020), bio-imaging and therapeutics (Hu et al., 2020), photocatalysis (Hutton, Martindale, & Reisner, 2017), adsorption treatment (Abd Rani, Ng, Ng, & Mahmoudi, 2020), etc. Ascribed to the multimodal applications of CDs, multiple synthesis methods such as the microwave, hydrothermal, ultrasonic and electrochemical methods, have been applied to synthesizing CDs. Due to its convenience and efficiency, the hydrothermal method has gained the most attention. In the hydrothermal method process, the precursor and solvent are two core factors that can affect the quantum yields (QYs) and emission properties of the obtained CDs. Different precursors can supply different surface functional groups on CDs, such as amines and carbonyl. These groups can operate excitation energy traps and cause large variations in fluorescence emission (Liu et al., 2016). Green precursors which contain a variety of ingredients can supply various tunable functional groups on the prepared CDs, resulting the improved photoluminescence properties of CDs and sensitivity for the analysis, which also meet the requirements of green chemistry standards and sustainable development. Thus, it is desirable and significant to prepare CDs using green precursors.

Green sources of CDs, especially traditional Chinese medicines (TCMs) and functional foods have gained much attention as they contain many active ingredients (e.g. flavonoids, alkaloids, anthraquinones, and coumarins) with abundant functional groups. These ingredients with various functional groups facilitate prepared CDs with good fluorescence properties, water solubility and bioavailability, even with some of the CDs having some biological activity (Chen, Ye, Yang, & Li, 2019). In our previous study, the flowers of Abelmoschus manihot (Linn.) Medicus (a typical TCM and a widely used tea in folk) have been prepared into CDs by hydrothermal method. The CDs exhibited low cytotoxicity and good water solubility with strong blue emission fluorescence (Wan, Wang, Zhang, Fu, Gao et al., 2019; Wan, Wang, Zhang, Fu, Wang et al., 2019). The successful preparation of flavonoid moiety-incorporated CDs with supersensitive selectivity demonstrates that TCMs are potential green precursors for CD preparation (J. Xu et al., 2018). Therefore, more these sources need to be exploited for their potential as green precursors for the CDs’ preparation. Sophora flavescens Aiton, called “Kushen” in Chinese, was reported to have a variety of pharmacological activities including antitumor, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory and antifungal activities (Park et al., 2020). Moreover, Kushen tea is usually used for clearing heat-fire in folk. Various ingredients such as flavonoids (e.g. 8-lavandulylkaempferol and trifolirhizin) (Yang et al., 2013), alkaloids (e.g. matrine and oxymatrine) (Funaya & Haginaka, 2012) etc. have made S. flavescens Aiton a good candidate for preparing CDs. Until now, S. flavescens Aiton has not been studied as a precursor for preparing CDs. Therefore, it is of academic interest to explore its potential use as a precursor.

In addition, it is noteworthy that the solvent is another core factor that affects the properties of CDs. Formamide, toluene, dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol, and diphenyl ether (Wang et al., 2020), etc. are notable reaction solvent for obtaining CDs with high QYs. However, their toxicity and environmental unfriendliness are factors inhibiting their use. In preparing of CDs with green precursors, water has been widely used as a green solvent (Sun et al., 2017). Most often, the use of water allows the prepared CDs to have good fluorescence properties and water solubility. However, the solubility in water of medium and small polar ingredients may hinder the fluorescence QY of the prepared CDs. In particular, when water is used as a solvent in preparing CDs using complex precursor ingredients, the possible active ingredients are not carbonized to form CDs if the active ingredients do not dissolve well in water. Therefore, it is very important to find a new green solvent that is widely soluble in preparing of CDs.

Deep eutectic solvents (DESs), a newly emerged solvent, have the properties of low cost, nontoxicity, biodegradability, easy preparation, wide dissolution range and good biocompatibility (Yu, Yue, Xu, & Jiang, 2020). They are eutectic mixtures composed of a hydrogen-bonding acceptor (HBA) and hydrogen-bonding donor (HBD) according to the hydrogen bonding (Zhang, De Oliveira Vigier, Royer, & Jérôme, 2012). DESs have the outstanding ability to dissolve a variety of compounds as they are capable of donating and accepting protons and electrons. Thus, DESs have been widely used as solvents in fields such as synthesis, separation science, and catalysis (Altunay, Elik, & Gürkan, 2020). Recently, DESs as solvents for preparing CDs have gained attention because they are green solvents that can replace water. The advantages of DESs over water can be summarized as follows: 1) DESs have good solubility for various ingredients. In the process of preparing CDs, more abundant components can be involved in the preparation process to improve the fluorescence performance of the generated CDs. 2) Some DESs can be used as doping agents to bring in sources such as nitrogen to participate in preparing CDs, resulting in the acquisition of CDs with higher QY (Jiang et al., 2018). To date, several DESs (Çalhan, Alaş, Aşık, Kaya, & Genç, 2018; Jiang et al., 2018, 2020), have already been used as solvents and doping agents for CD preparation. The obtained CDs show outstanding fluorescence and detection performance, demonstrating that DESs have great potential in preparing CDs. Moreover, DESs have a good extraction effect on the active ingredients (alkaloids and flavonoids) (Jiang et al., 2019) and DESs (solvent and doping agent) have not yet been combined with TCMs or functional food (precursors) to prepare CDs until now. The combination of these may have good potential to prepare CDs with outstanding properties.

The research by Steinmetz and Potter in the early 1980s showed the relationship between a diet high in fruits and vegetables and a reduced risk of chronic diseases; since, the flavonoid content in foods have attracted great attraction (Harnly et al., 2006). Among them, myricetin is an effective ingredient in many foods and Chinese medicines, such as blueberries, vine teas, and A. manihot (Linn.) Medicus flowers, and was reported to have the potential to serve as a functional food ingredient with many biological activities, such as antidiabetic, antioxidative, anticarcinogen, antibacterial, neuroprotective, and hepatoprotective activities. (Arumugam, Palanisamy, Chua, & Kuppusamy, 2016, 2019). In addition to inhibiting platelet aggregation, myricetin has rich anti-inflammatory and anti-cancer pharmacological activities (Tan et al., 2018). Moreover, myricetin can be used in preparing cosmetics by inhibiting the generation of hydrogen peroxide in cells induced with ultraviolet B to quench the photoaging free radicals in the skin (Guo, Fu, Chen, Zhou, & Chen, 2016). However, fluorescence and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) methods to detect myricetin content are still very few.

Herein, using S. flavescens Aiton as a green precursor, an efficient method for synthesizing CDs with the assistance of DES was developed for the first time. To further improve the fluorescence properties and selective sensitivity, CDs were further surface modified using polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a hydrophilic polymer (CDs@PEI) (Hao et al., 2016). Moreover, the number of hydrophilic groups on PEI may improve the water solubility and stability of CDs (Cheng, Xing, & Wu, 2019). Methods such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Zeta potential analysis, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to characterize the obtained CDs and CDs@PEI. Systematic studies on the fluorescence properties and optical properties of CDs and CDs@PEI were carried out. Furthermore, CDs@PEI was applied as a fluorescence sensor to detect myricetin from A. manihot (Linn.) Medicus flowers, vine teas and blueberries, and the sensing results were satisfactory. Finally, CDs@PEI was applied in cell imaging in HepG2 cells to explore its potential in the field of imaging.
The chemicals and instrument information are shown in Electronic Supporting Material.

DESsweresynthesizedbya heatingmethodaccording to a reported procedure (Wan, Wang, Zhang, Fu, Gao et al., 2019; Wan, Wang, Zhang, Fu, Wang et al., 2019).Briefly,choline chloride (ChCl) and tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB) as HBAs were mixed with the HBD (urea, malonate, acetamide, glycerin and malic acid) in a specific molar ratio and were placed in a reaction reagent bottle. The optimized DES for preparing CDs was composed of ChCl (13.96 g) and urea (12.01 g) with a molar ratio of 1:2. The mixtures werevigorouslystirred(300 rpm)at 80 °Cinan oilbathuntil a uniform liquid eutectic mixture of approximately 30 mL was formed.

CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. First, 2 mL of deionized water and 18 mL of obtained DES were added to 2 g of S. flavescens Aiton powder and ultrasonicated for 30 min. Then, the mixture was kept in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at an optimized temperature of 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the upper solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and then filtered to remove fluorescent large insoluble particles through a 0.22 μm filter (Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd). The solution was further dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (3000 D tubing) for 48 h. The types of DES, water content in DES, amounts of S. flavescens Aiton, reaction time, and reaction temperature were optimized to obtain CDs with higher QY.

CDs@PEI was synthesized using PEI to modify the surface of CDs through a one-step hydrothermal treatment. Briefly, 5 mg PEI was dissolved into the CDs aqueous solution and transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, 10 min of centrifugation at 10000 rpm and 48 h of dialysis were used to purify the product.

To study the sensitivity of CDs and CDs@PEI, various concentrations of myricetin (0.3 mL) with a range of 0–100 μM were added to 2.7 mL solution of CDs (12 μg·mL−1) or CDs@PEI (14 μg⋅mL−1) and measured using a fluorescence spectrometer at room temperature. In addition, the structural analogs of myricetin (structures of compounds are shown in Fig. S1), and some ions were selected to explore the selectivity of CDs@PEI. F0/F was used to evaluate the fluorescence quenching efficiency of myricetin, where F0 and F refer to the integrated fluorescence intensities of CDs and CDs@PEI in the absence and presence of myricetin, respectively.

The DES, which was prepared using choline chloride (ChCl) and urea (molar ratio of 1:2), was used to extract the myricetin from A. manihot (Linn.) Medicus flowers, vine teas, and blueberries. First, the blueberries were threshed into small pieces and dehydrated in an oven at 40 °C until dry and crispy. Then, the dried blueberries, A. manihot (Linn.) manihot (Linn.) Medicus flowers and vine teas were ground into powders, and samples were stored in re-sealable bags in a dry place. Then, 1.8 mL DES and 0.2 mL water were injected into a 5 mL centrifuge tube with 15 mg powdered sample. Then, the extraction was conducted in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min at 40 °C. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min and filtration with a 0.22 μm membrane filter, the supernatant of the extraction was applied for further fluorescence analysis and HPLC analysis.

To verify the accuracy of the analysis results obtained by CDs@PEI, the quantification analysis of extract samples was also performed using an Agilent 1260 series HPLC system (Agilent Technologies). Chromatographic analysis was performed on a commercial Agilent TC-C18 analytical column (250 mm × 4.6 μm., 5 μm particle size). A mixture of 30% methanol and 70% acetic acid aqueous solution (0.2%) was used as the mobile phase. The column temperature was maintained at 35 °C and the flow rate was set to 0.8 mL min−1. Myricetin was detected at a wavelength of 370 nm with an injection volume of 10 μL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs and CDs@PEI in living cells

ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability was measured on cancer cells (HepG2) upon the uptake of CDs and CDs@PEI. Initially, HepG2 cells were seeded into 96-well plates and then incubated in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 24 h to ensure that the cells attached to the wells. Then, various concentrations of CDs and CDs@PEI were added into each well and incubated for another 24 h. Next, 10 μL of MTT (5.0 mg mL−1) was added into each well. After a further incubation time of 4 h, the supernatant in all wells was removed and 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well to dissolve the formazan complex. The plate was shaken before testing. Finally, the optical absorbance was measured to calculate the cell viability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging of CDs and CDs@PEI
ce:section.ce:para: HepG2 cells were cultured in DMEM medium (containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS)) in 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h until confluency. Then, the cells were treated with 0.3 mg mL−1 (adding 10 μL) of CDs and CDs@PEI. After incubation for 0, 1, 2, 4, 6 and 8 h, the DMEM medium was discarded and HepG2 cells were washed three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffer (pH = 7.4) to remove extracellular CDs and CDs@PEI. Finally, the cells were kept in PBS buffer for cell imaging.

DESs as environmentally friendly media can be used for preparing CDs (Çalhan et al., 2018). To investigate the influence of DES formed by different components on the QY of CDs, different HBA (ChCl and TBAB) and HBD (urea, malonate, acetamide, glycerin and malic acid) were used to synthesize the DES and prepare CDs. Moreover, the molarratio of HBA and HBD for DES preparation was optimized. Since some proportions of HBA and HBD failed to synthesize liquid DESs (Fig S2 and Table S1), we only investigated the influence of successfully synthesized liquid DESs on the preparation of CDs. It was found that CDs prepared using ChCl as HBA and urea as HBD with a molar ratio of 1:2 for the DES had a higher QY. Therefore, this DES was used to prepare CDs for further experiments. It has been reported that DESs with a certain water content have a good extraction effect on the active ingredients (alkaloids and flavonoids) (Jiang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018). Moreover, as the extraction solvent, DESs have the disadvantage of excessive viscosity; the viscosity of DES can be effectively adjusted by changing the proportion of water in the solvent to improve the extraction efficiency of DES. Therefore, the factors (including thewater content of the solvent, dosageof S. flavescens Aiton, reaction temperature, reaction time), which may influence the QY of CDs, were optimized. The highest QY obtained was 31.64% using DES with 10% water content as the extraction solvent, 2 g of S. flavescens Aiton as the precursor, 180 °C as the reaction temperature, and 4 h as the reaction time (shown in Tables S1 and S2).

The general scheme for the preparation process and applications of CDs and CDs@PEI is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Since the surface functional groups of the obtained CDs are abundant and easy to modify, some organic molecules or polymers can be used to modify CDs to improve their fluorescence performance. PEI, a hydrophilic polymer with many active groups on its molecular structure, can form strong hydrogen bonds with organic compounds containing oxygen or nitrogen functional groups (Hao et al., 2016). A large number of hydrophilic groups on PEI may improve the water solubility and stability of CDs (Cheng et al., 2019). Moreover, the PEI- functionalized CDs exhibited good biocompatibility and increased cellular uptake due to the high transfection efficiency of positively charged nanoparticles (Shahid, Mohiyuddin, & Packirisamy, 2020).

The spherical morphologies of CDs and CDs@PEI were measured by TEM and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Fig. 1 A and B indicate that the prepared CDs and CDs@PEI are spherical in morphology and well dispersed in the aqueous medium. In addition, the HRTEM images (inset in Fig. 1A and B) show the crystalline structure of CDs and CDs@PEI. As shown in Figs. S3A and 1C, the average diameter of CDs and CDs@PEI were estimated to be approximately 2.82 nm and 2.97 nm, respectively. Figs. S3B and 1D show the patterns of EDS measurement of the CDs and CDs@PEI, respectively, indicating that CDs are composed of carbon 97.61 (98.1%), nitrogen 0.85 (0.74%), oxygen 1.54 (1.16%), while CDs@PEI is composed of carbon 90.6 (92.45%), nitrogen 3.24 (2.84%) and oxygen 6.16 (4.72%). The N content in CDs@PEI increases compared with the elemental content of CDs, indicating that PEI was successfully modified on the surface of the CDs.

The surface groups, states and crystalline properties of CDs and CDs@PEI were further investigated by FT-IR spectrum, XPS analysis, XRD pattern, and Zeta potential (Electronic Supporting Material). In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. S4A), the peaks at 3382 cm−1, 1656 cm−1 and 1619 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH, CC, and CO, respectively. The bands at 1467 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the CH and CN bands. Moreover, compared with CDs, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs@PEI shows an increased NH band at 2895 cm−1 and a strong characteristic band of C-OH at 1095 cm−1 which belongs to the PEI (Cheng et al., 2019; Gao, Li, Shi, & Yang, 2019). Furthermore, XPS analysis was conducted to investigate the surface element states of CDs@PEI. As shown in Fig. S4B, three predominant peaks for C1s (285.06 eV), N1s (399.74 eV), and O1s (530.07 eV) are identified in the XPS spectra of the survey scan. The spectrum of C1s (Fig. S5A) can be divided into four peaks for CC/CO, CO/CCl, CN and CO bands at 283.9 eV, 284.5 eV, 285.4 eV and 287.4 eV, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S5B) contains two peaks at 398.9 and 401.5 eV, which can be assigned to CN and NH groups, respectively. The O1s spectra (Fig. S5C) shows two peaks corresponding to two oxygen species: CO (530.3 eV) and C-OH (531.5 eV) (Wang et al., 2018). Moreover, the XRD pattern of CDs@PEI was used to confirm its crystalline nature. It can be seen that there is a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 28.03°in the XRD pattern (Fig. S4C), revealing that CDs@PEI contains a high disorder of carbon atoms.

Further, the surface charges of CDs, PEI, and CDs@PEI were also studied and are displayed in Fig. S4D. As shown, CDs have a Zeta potential of −3.37 mV, while PEI is approximately 9.72 mV. After surface modification of PEI, CDs@PEI showed a positive potential of 4.19 mV. The change in charge might enhance cellular uptake (Shahid et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence properties of CDs and CDs@PEI
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 2 A, it is obvious that CDs and CDs@PEI show an absorption peak around 270–280 nm in the UV domain of the electromagnetic radiation with the tail stretching into the visible domain. An obvious absorption peak of CDs appeared at 276 nm, which was assigned to the n-π* transition of the CO structure on the CDs (Jo & Yang, 2016). After passivation with PEI, the absorption of CDs increased to a longer wavelength (281 nm). This deviation is mainly due to the difference in the structure and composition of CDs and CDs@PEI. The appearance of the absorption peaks of CD and CDs@PEI in the same domain and the increased absorption peak of CDs@PEI confirmed the successful modification of PEI on the surface of CDs (Shahid et al., 2020). Figs. S6A and 2B reveal the fluorescent properties of CDs and CDs@PEI. As illustrated, both CDs and CDs@PEI exhibited maximal fluorescence emission intensity at 450 nm when the excitation wavelength was 370 nm. In addition, Figs. S6A and 2B, it was almost colorless under normal daylight and emitted a bright blue fluorescence under UV illumination of the CDs and CDs@PEI solutions (inset in Figs. S6A and 2B). The different emission wavelengths of the CDs and CDs@PEI with the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 410 nm are shown in Figs. S6B and 2C. The maximum fluorescence emission was observed encountered at λex/λem = 370/450 nm. Furthermore, a red shift in the emission wavelength is observed with an increase in the excitation wavelength, as shown in the corresponding normalized spectra (Fig. 2D). This phenomenon of this excitation-dependent emission behavior is due to the different states on the surface of CDs@PEI which are caused by the different functional groups on the CDs@PEI’ surface and different sizes of CDs@PEI. Furthermore, the fluorescence emitting ability of CDs@PEI at 450 nm was studied by the Commission International de L’Eclairage (CIE), (coordinates at (0.15, 0.12) in Fig. 2E), which confirmed the blue-light fluorescence of the CDs@PEI (Chandra et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of myricetin on the fluorescence properties of CDs and CDs@PEI
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of evaluating the effect of myricetin on the fluorescence properties of CDs/CDs@PEI, factors including the solvent, stability, pH, ionic strength, incubation time and concentration of CDs/CDs@PEI were investigated. Fig. S7A-F show the respective results (given in the Electronic Supporting Material). These results illustrate that the synthesized CDs/CDs@PEI have good solubility and stability and can be used to detect myricetin in physiological environments (pH = 7.4) with salt tolerance. Moreover, the incubation time of the CDs/CDs@PEI to myricetin was evaluated in 60 min, and the stable fluorescence response intensity at no more than 2 min of incubation time (Fig. S7E) indicates that the process of fluorescence quenching between CDs/CDs@PEI and myricetin is rapid. Fig. S7F shows that the optimal concentrations of CDs and CDs@PEI are 12 μg·mL−1 and14 μg·mL−1, respectively. Therefore, CDs at a concentration of 12 μg·mL−1 and CDs@PEI at a concentration of 14 μg·mL−1 were used in subsequent experiments.

The fluorescence response of CDs and CDs@PEI was investigated by adding 300 μL myricetin at different concentrations to the 2.7 mL CDs/CDs@PEI solution under optimum conditions. As indicated in Figs. S8 and 3 A, the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs and CDs@PEI gradually decreased as the concentration of myricetin increased from 0 to 100 μM. As shown in Fig. 3B, in the range of 0–100 μM, a linear relationship with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9589 between F0/F of CDs and the myricetin concentration were obtained by repeating the same experiments five times. The linear regression was calculated using the equation: F0/F = 0.0359[c] + 0.8517. A good linear relationship of CDs@PEI was obtained with an R2 of 0.9613, and the linear equation was expressed as F0/F = 0.0395[c] + 0.8747 in the same range of myricetin. The limit of detection (LOD) is the concentration of myricetin required to quench the blank signal of the CDs/CDs@PEI solution based on the generalized three times standard deviation of 11 measurements. The LOD of CDs@PEI for myricetin was 10 nM, which was lower than the LOD of CDs (0.1 μM). This is the first study using the fluorescence method to detect myricetin, and the LOD of CDs@PEI is very satisfactory. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. 3B that the same concentration of myricetin has a stronger fluorescence quenching effect on CDs @PEI than on CDs, which corresponds to the higher QY of CDs@PEI. The overall outcome indicated that CDs@PEI is a reliable fluorescence probe for detecting myricetin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of the CDs@PEI - myricetin system
ce:section.ce:para:
Formation of ground-state complexes, collision quenching, molecular rearrangement, energy transfer, molecular interactions and excited-state reactions may all lead to quenching of fluorescence intensity. The resulting quenching mechanisms, such as electron transfer (ET), fluorescence response energy transfer (FRET), and the inner filter effect (IFE) have been used to identify the multifarious targets. Moreover, static and dynamic quenching are two types of fluorescence quenching mechanisms (Xu, Chen, & Ma, 2018). When a non-fluorescence ground-state complex is formed and the lifetime of the fluorescence molecule in the excited state does not change in the presence of the quencher, static quenching occurs. However, if the fluorescence lifetime is shortened due to collision between the fluorophore and the quencher molecule, dynamic quenching takes place. (Xu et al., 2018). Therefore, we first measured the fluorescence lifetime of CDs@PEI. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs@PEI and CDs@PEI/myricetin are 12.1 ns and 11.8 ns, respectively (Fig. 4 A). The slightly changed fluorophore lifetime after adding myricetin demonstrates the possibility of IFE between CDs@PEI and myricetin. The Stern-Volmer equation, expressed as follows can be used to show the fluorescence quenching:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
c
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
c

The τ0 is the average lifetime of CDs@PEI. Kq refers to the quenching rate constant, while Ksv is the quenching constant. Kq and Ksv were obtained by carrying out the detection at different temperatures. If Ksv decreases as the reaction temperature increases, static quenching may occur. As shown in Fig. 4B and Table S3, as the reaction temperature increases from 25 °C to 45 °C, the Ksv of CDs @ PEI gradually decreases. In addition, the Kq are 1.95 × 1012 L·mol−1·s−1(at 25 °C), 1.84 × 1012 L·mol−1·s−1(at 35 °C), and 1.78 × 1012 L·mol−1·s−1(at 45 °C), respectively. It is reported that the maximum Kq of collision quenching is 1–2 × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1 (Gan, Liu, Liu, & Hu, 2012). All Kqs in this experiment far exceeded this value, which further proves that the fluorescence quenching of myricetin on CDs@PEI contributes to static quenching (Cui, Song, Wang, & Song, 2015).

As static quenching is usually caused by the state complex, the hard and soft acids and bases theory (HSAB) was used to confirm the formation of the complex (Wu & Chen, 2014). According to the HSAB theory, strong acids (phenolic hydroxyl groups) of myricetin might interact strongly with soft bases (high polarizable donor atom) of CDs@PEI and form a conjugation of CDs@PEI- myricetin complex. Since this complex might prevent the transfer of non-radiative electrons to holes and promote the release of reduced recombinant fluorescence, the concentration of fluorescent molecules may decrease. The explanation of HSAB theory further confirmed that the fluorescence quenching progress of CDs@PEI is static, which concurs with the Stern-Volmer plot.

Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectra of myricetin and the fluorescence spectra of CDs@PEI were compared. As shown in Fig. 4C, the maximum absorption peak of myricetin overlaps with the excitation spectra of CDs@PEI. The IFE occurs when the excitation spectra of the fluorescence material possess an obvious overlapped region with the absorption spectra of the quencher or analyte. Therefore, IFE might be a possible mechanism between myricetin and the fluorescence of CDs@PEI. The overall outcome suggests that the CDs@PEI fluorescence quenching can be attributed to the IFE mechanism and is a static quenching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study of CDs and CDs@PEI
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity assay was carried out to investigate the sensing performance of CDs and CDs@PEI to myricetin using various ions and competitive compounds. Myricetin 0.1 mM and other flavonoids with similar chemical structures analogous to myricetin (apigenin, quercetin, nobiletin, diosmetin, rutin, kaempferol and luteolin, compound structures are shown in Fig. S1), or other ions (Al3+, Na+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+,CO3 2–,SO4 2- and S2O3 2-) were added to the work solutions under optimal conditions. As seen in Fig. S9A and B, the interference (metal ions, anions and analogous) has a negligible quenching effect on the CDs and CDs@PEI. However, due to the existence of the IFE mechanism in the case of myricetin while no such mechanism prevailed for other co-existing flavonoid compounds and ions, myricetin exhibited an excellent fluorescence quenching effect. These results suggest that CDs and CDs@PEI are ideal fluorescence materials that can be used for highly selective myricetin detection in the presence of interfering substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of myricetin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: CDs@PEI was applied for myricetin extract detection from A. manihot (Linn.) Medicus flowers, vine teas, and blueberries using DES as the extraction solvent to determine the practicality of CDs@PEI. The myricetin concentrations in three samples were 3.56 μM, 27.49 μM and 92.62 μM, using the as-prepared CDs@PEI sensor, which is consistent with that obtained by the HPLC method (Table 1 ). Real sample recovery tests were then carried out by spiking a series of known concentrations of myricetin using this fluorescence method. The recovery percentage of myricetin in these real samples ranged between 97.88 and 107.08%. The observed results further reveal that CDs@PEI has good analytical performance and can be used to detect myricetin in practical samples.

To apply the CDs/CDs@PEI in cell imaging, the toxicity and biocompatibility between CDs/CDs@PEI and cells were first evaluated by a MTT assay using HepG2 cells. The cell viability results were obtained after the cells were incubated in CDs/CDs@PEI solution at different concentrations (0 to 0.3 mg mL−1). It is shown that the cell viability is more than 85% after 24 h incubation when the concentration of CDs/CDs@PEI reach 0.3 mg mL−1 (Fig. 5 A). The results reveal the low toxicity and favorable biocompatibility of CDs and CDs@PEI. Moreover, the PEI-passivated CDs (CDs@PEI) exhibited higher biocompatibility, which may be attributed to the successful formation of CDs from S. flavescens Aiton, which contains organic compounds. The enhanced biocompatibility of CDs@PEI ensures the efficiency of bio-imaging.

CDs and CDs@PEI were used for cell imaging. Fig. 5B shows that the cells did not emit fluorescence before adding CDs and CDs@PEI. After incubation for 8 h with CDs/CDs@PEI (Fig. 5C and D), the treated cells emitted blue and the CDs@PEI treated cells showed more fluorescence than in case of CDs-treated cells. Therefore, CDs@PEI was chosen to perform the cellular uptake study by incubating HepG2 cells with 0.3 mg mL−1 of CDs@PEI for 0, 1, 2, 4, 6 and 8 h. Fig. S10 illustrates that the fluorescence intensity of HepG2 cells increased with increased incubation time up to 4 h. The small size of CDs@PEI enables the successful uptake of CDs@PEI into HepG2 cells. Subsequently, as the incubation time increased, a decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed, which might be due to the excretion of CDs@PEI out of the cells by metabolism (Wang et al., 2018). Therefore, the incubation time of cell imaging was optimized as 4 h for cellular uptake and the results indicate that the prepared CDs@PEI has potential for bio-application in fluorescence imaging.

In summary, CDs with high QY were synthesized using urea and S. flavescens Aiton as the precursor and DES as both the eco-friendly solvent and doping agent. The PEI-passivated CDs exhibited increased fluorescence intensity and good solubility and were used as a novel and sensitive fluorescence probe for myricetin through the IFE mechanism for the first time. Under optimal conditions, CDs@PEI showed higher sensitivity for myricetin detection over a wide concentration range from 0 to 100 μM with a LOD of 10 nM. Furthermore, CDs@PEI was applied for the analysis of myricetin extracted from A. manihot (Linn.) Medicus flowers, vine teas and blueberries using DES as an extraction solvent. Finally, the cell imaging and time-dependent cellular uptake studies of the CDs@PEI with enhanced biocompatibility confirmed that the CDs@PEI can be used as an efficient imaging probe for application in the bio-imaging field and even diagnostics.

Min Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Validation. Xun Kang: Writing - original draft. Linlin Deng: . Min Wang: . Zhining Xia: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Die Gao: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21904109 and No. 21974015) and the Luzhou Science and Technology Bureau Innovation Miao sub-project (NO. 2019-RCM-92).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.128817.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
